articles.title: The ‘Adventures of Cliff Booth’ Release Date Has Been Revealed. Here's Everything We Know
articles.why: David Fincher directing a sequel to Once Upon a Time in Hollywood — with an IMAX theatrical release before Netflix — is one of the most unexpected film announcements in years, pairing the most meticulous living director with Tarantino's most personal universe.
articles.hook: David Fincher is making the Cliff Booth movie. / IMAX first. Netflix after. / This is real.

articles.title: ‘Cliff Booth’ Movie Starring Brad Pitt To Bow In Imax Theaters On Thanksgiving Weekend Before Heading To Netflix
articles.why: Brad Pitt returning to the Once Upon a Time in Hollywood universe — getting an IMAX Thanksgiving slot before landing on Netflix — is one of the most anticipated film announcements of the year, and the window strategy signals Netflix is learning how to treat prestige movies.
articles.hook: Cliff Booth is back. / IMAX Thanksgiving. / Then Netflix.

articles.title: ‘Perfect Storm’: How Trump’s Aid Cuts Are Fueling the Ebola Outbreak
articles.why: The Ebola outbreak already cleared the bar as a major story, and this piece adds a specific, damning new angle: US aid cuts are directly creating supply shortages of basic protective equipment on the ground, accelerating containment failure.
articles.hook: No masks. No hand sanitizer. / The funding got cut / Now Ebola spreads.

articles.title: U.S. crude oil falls below $100 per barrel after Trump says Iran talks in final stages
articles.why: Oil falling below $100 on Trump's claim that Iran talks are in their final stages is a market-moving data point that could signal a genuine de-escalation — or a premature one — in the conflict that has been driving global inflation all year.
articles.hook: Oil dropped below $100 / Trump says Iran talks are almost done / The bond market isn't convinced yet.

articles.title: Intuit plans to cut workforce by about 17% as tax software maker reckons with slowing growth
articles.why: Intuit cutting 17% of its workforce while explicitly naming AI as both the disruptive threat and the strategic bet is the clearest case yet of a major software company acknowledging that generative AI is cannibalizing its own product category.
articles.hook: Intuit is cutting 17% of its staff / Because AI might replace what they sell / They're betting on AI anyway.

articles.title: Bad Bunny Is Launching the Benito Antonio Collection, Rooted in Identity & Culture, With This Fashion Retailer
articles.why: Already covered — Bad Bunny's 150-piece Zara collection was featured in a previous run as a brand-scale launch, not a typical collab.

articles.title: Harry Styles’ Team Responds to Fan Complaints About Obstructed ‘Together, Together’ Tour Sight Lines
articles.why: Harry Styles' team publicly addressing sightline complaints from his opening tour night — and committing to stage changes — is a meaningful signal about the power dynamic between artists and fans in the post-pandemic arena era, and a real-time test of his goodwill.
articles.hook: Harry Styles' first tour show. / Fans couldn't see. / His team is already fixing the stage.

articles.title: Everlane Sold to Shein. Was It Ever Going to Make Fashion Better?
articles.why: Already covered — the Everlane-Shein story was featured in a previous run as a symbol of ethical fashion's limits.

articles.title: Ariana Grande Drops Dangerous Woman Outtake “Knew Better Part Two”
articles.why: Already covered — Ariana Grande's Dangerous Woman anniversary edition was featured in a previous run.

articles.title: Billy Joel Doesn’t Want You to See the Billy Joel Biopic
articles.why: Already covered — Billy Joel's rep denouncing the unauthorized biopic was featured in a previous run.

articles.title: Arab Strap Launch New Album Half-Told Tales With Acerbic Single “You You You”
articles.why: Arab Strap burying a pointed line about Spotify funding 'weapons-grade AI' inside a new single is the kind of lyrical provocation that captures a real tension in the music industry — artists being forced to distribute through platforms actively building the tools that could replace them.
articles.hook: Arab Strap's new song calls out Spotify directly. / 'We both fund weapons-grade AI.' / It's in the lyrics.

articles.title: Pizza Hut Bets on 1980s Nostalgia to Survive $100M AI Lawsuit
articles.why: Pizza Hut leaning into 1980s nostalgia while simultaneously facing a $100M AI lawsuit is a vivid collision of retro marketing and tech accountability — a real story about what happens when AI breaks something beloved.
articles.hook: Pizza Hut got sued $100M over AI. / Their fix? Go full 1980s.

articles.title: Selena Gomez’s Genre-Defying 'X-Rated' Movie Has Cannes Buzzing
articles.why: Selena Gomez taking a bold swing at a genre-defying Cannes film alongside Cate Blanchett and Michael Fassbender is a meaningful career pivot that has the festival talking.
articles.hook: Selena Gomez. Cate Blanchett. Cannes. / Nobody saw this coming.

articles.title: Walé Adeyemi Helped Define An Era Of Black British Style. Now He’s Back To Do It Again.
articles.why: Walé Adeyemi helped shape UK garage and Black British style from Camden Market — his relaunch is a genuine cultural homecoming for one of fashion's underacknowledged architects.
articles.hook: He built Black British style in the '90s. / Now he's back.

articles.title: Major Broadway & West End Theater Owner ATG Entertainment In Early Stages Of Possible Sale – Report
articles.why: ATG Entertainment controls some of Broadway and the West End's most iconic venues — a potential sale would be one of the largest shifts in live theater ownership in decades.
articles.hook: The company behind Book of Mormon and Harry Potter on stage / might be for sale.

articles.title: ‘Victorian Psycho’ Director on the Challenge of Making a ‘Demented’ and ‘Funny’ Movie Where You Root for Maika Monroe’s Serial Killer
articles.why: Victorian Psycho — Maika Monroe as a Victorian serial killer you're meant to root for — is one of Cannes' more brazenly concept-driven entries, and the director's explanation of how to make murder funny and sympathetic is worth the read.
articles.hook: Maika Monroe plays a Victorian serial killer. / You're supposed to root for her.

articles.title: Paramount’s Junk-Status Credit Rating to Be Downgraded Further Following Warner Bros. Merger to Reflect ‘Major Ongoing Uncertainties,’ S&P Global Says
articles.why: The Paramount-Warner Bros. merger is one of the most consequential media consolidations in decades, and S&P signaling a deeper junk-status downgrade adds a concrete financial alarm to what was already a structurally fragile deal. This is the kind of credit signal that quietly reshapes how Hollywood operates.
articles.hook: Paramount-WBD merger hits credit wall / S&P says junk gets junkier

articles.title: Who Can Be Trusted When Everyone Is Vulnerable?
articles.why: Two OnlyFans-themed prestige TV shows debuting in the same season is a genuine cultural signal about how mainstream the creator economy has become — and this review uses the coincidence to ask something sharper about intimacy, screens, and trust.
articles.hook: Two OnlyFans shows. Same season. / Nobody planned this.

articles.title: What ‘Survivor’ Taught America About Truth and Power
articles.why: Rolling Stone's argument that Survivor taught America that truth doesn't matter — only the compelling story does — lands as a sharp piece of cultural criticism that explains something real about how we got here. The show as origin myth for post-truth politics is a genuinely interesting frame.
articles.hook: Survivor taught us truth doesn't matter / Just tell a better story

articles.title: Vibe coding is coming to your phone
articles.why: Google bringing vibe coding — the ability to build your own apps through natural language prompts — directly to Android home screens is the consumer-facing version of a shift that's been building in developer circles for a year. When it lands on your phone, it stops being a tech story.
articles.hook: Build your own app with a prompt / Coming to your home screen.

articles.title: You can now remix other people’s YouTube Shorts with AI
articles.why: YouTube letting users remix other people's Shorts with AI — inserting themselves into existing videos — is a meaningful creative and legal inflection point. It normalizes AI-generated derivative content at massive scale, with implications for creator ownership that haven't been resolved.
articles.hook: YouTube lets you insert yourself into anyone's video / AI does the rest.

articles.title: I Gave My OpenClaw Agent a Physical Body
articles.why: AI coding abilities are now being used to build and deploy physical robots — a meaningful convergence of software and hardware that signals how quickly the robotics barrier to entry is falling.
articles.hook: You can now give your AI agent a body / The coding skills already exist / The robots are being built

articles.title: Hypershell X Ultra S Review: The Best Exoskeleton Yet
articles.why: Consumer exoskeletons crossing into genuinely wearable territory — without the uncanny marionette feeling — is a quiet milestone in assistive technology that deserves attention as the category matures.
articles.hook: It powers your legs / Without making you feel like a puppet

articles.title: Yearslong fight over users' right to tweak smart TV software heads to trial
articles.why: A multi-year legal fight over whether Vizio must share the Linux source code running on its smart TVs is a surprisingly consequential case for user rights, open-source software, and how much control manufacturers can retain over devices you own.
articles.hook: You bought the TV / They own the software running on it / That fight is finally going to trial.

articles.title: Moose-proof and megacasting: Ars drives the new Volvo EX60
articles.why: The Volvo EX60 is the first model built entirely on Volvo's own EV platform — a moose-tested, megacast electric SUV that signals how quickly European automakers are racing to build their own EV foundations independent of legacy architecture.
articles.hook: Volvo's first ground-up electric / Moose-tested in Barcelona / The V8 era is over.

articles.title: E.l.f. Beauty to walk back some tariff price increases amid high gas prices and consumer 'suffering'
articles.why: E.l.f. Beauty rolling back tariff-driven price increases because consumers are pulling back is a small but concrete signal that the consumer economy is starting to push back against inflation — a data point that tells you something real about the spending mood right now.
articles.hook: They raised prices for tariffs / Now customers stopped buying / The rollback begins.
